one_line_takeaway: A game-theoretically grounded benchmark showing frontier LLMs pick collectively harmful actions in 38% of high-stakes multi-agent scenarios, with mechanism-design prompt interventions (mediation best) recovering up to +0.18 in social-welfare accuracy.
key_finding: Across 15 frontier models and 1,535 scenarios, models choose the socially optimal action in only 62% of cases (fail 38%). Failures are structure-dependent: in Prisoner's Dilemma both agents cooperate only 44% of the time (lowest welfare), versus 80% cooperation in Chicken; even with aligned incentives, Battle of the Sexes coordination succeeds only 48%. Framing matters: surfacing explicit payoffs raises Nash-equilibrium accuracy +6.20% but drops utilitarian accuracy -4.06% (more self-interested play), and randomizing coordination option order costs 15% on average (only 5-6% for GPT-5-class models). In reasoning traces, payoff-maximization reasoning is strongly linked to suboptimal outcomes (Delta=-0.17) while welfare/safety reasoning is linked to optimal ones (Rawlsian Delta=+0.11, AI-safety Delta=+0.10, utilitarian Delta=+0.07). All five mechanism-design interventions improve utilitarian accuracy, from +0.13 (Contracts) to +0.18 (Mediator, best overall), though payments/penalties/mediator reduce Nash accuracy by -0.06. Capability does not monotonically predict social welfare; by family the ordering is Anthropic > Meta > OpenAI > Google/Qwen/DeepSeek/Grok.
core_contribution: GT-HarmBench is a benchmark of 1,535 high-stakes, natural-language multi-agent scenarios that each instantiate one of six canonical symmetric 2x2 games (Prisoner's Dilemma, Chicken, Stag Hunt, Battle of the Sexes, Coordination, No Conflict). The scenarios are derived by mapping real-world AI risks from the MIT AI Risk Repository onto game-theoretic structures, so the paper evaluates whether frontier LLMs, acting as strategic agents, choose socially beneficial actions in concrete high-stakes settings (e.g., military escalation, election manipulation, medical malpractice) rather than in abstract payoff games. Beyond diagnosis, the paper contributes an intervention study: five classical mechanism-design treatments (pre-play communication, commitment contracts, trusted mediator, penalty contracts, side payments) applied as prompt modifications to test whether they steer agents toward socially optimal outcomes. This is the paper's OWN framing; prior benchmarks it contrasts against (Table 1: Akata et al., GTBench, TMGBench, FAIRGAME, SanctSim, MoralSim, CoopEval) are positioning, not its contribution.
threat_model: Multi-agent AI safety failures: even individually well-intentioned, competent agents can produce collectively harmful outcomes through conflict (mutual defection) and coordination failures (miscoordination) when deployed in high-stakes strategic domains such as militaries, financial markets, and cybersecurity. The concern is that existing single-agent safety benchmarks cannot surface these interaction-induced failure modes. The 'attack surface' here is emergent strategic behavior rather than an adversary; the paper measures reliability gaps (choosing collectively harmful actions) and whether institutional mechanisms can mitigate them.
limitations: Main results use self-play, which the authors note underpredicts miscoordination in mixed-model (cross-play) settings; cross-play is relegated to an appendix. The entire pipeline (risk-to-game mapping, scenario generation, and filtering) is LLM-generated and LLM-judged (GPT-5.1), so benchmark validity rests on model-based construction, only partially offset by a small 30-scenario human study (2 annotators). Scenarios are static, single-shot 2x2 symmetric games; the design deliberately excludes asymmetric games (power/information disparities) and repeated/iterated dynamics, limiting generalization to realistic multi-round or asymmetric interactions. 'Social optimality' is operationalized via utilitarian welfare on generated payoff matrices, so results are only as meaningful as those synthetic payoffs. Mechanisms are simulated purely as prompt reframings ('reasons as if' under an institutional arrangement), not enforced game changes, so effects may reflect prompt sensitivity rather than genuine incentive shifts. Reasoning-pattern analysis is correlational and depends on a GPT-4o-mini judge.

datasets_used: GT-HarmBench (this paper): 1,535 scenarios across 6 games - Prisoner's Dilemma 490, Chicken 379, Stag Hunt 317, Coordination 180, Battle of the Sexes 141, No Conflict 28
MIT AI Risk Repository (Slattery et al. 2024): 1,612 valid entries; 604 (37.5%) classified as multi-actor strategic; 1,816 (risk, game) pairs generated
12,280 chain-of-thought decision traces from 4 models (Claude Sonnet 4.5, Claude Opus 4.5, Qwen3 30B, DeepSeek V3.2) for reasoning analysis
quantitative_results: Overall utilitarian accuracy across 15 models: 62% socially optimal (38% failure)

Prisoner's Dilemma cooperation: 44% (average utilitarian accuracy 0.46); Chicken: 80% (avg 0.78); Battle of the Sexes coordination: 48% (avg 0.46); Stag Hunt avg 0.60; Coordination avg 0.80; No Conflict avg 1.00

Weighted-average utilitarian accuracy per model: Claude 4.5 Opus 0.87 (best), Llama 3.3 70B 0.81, Claude 4.5 Sonnet 0.79, GPT-4o 0.75, Llama 3.2 3B 0.72, GPT-5.1 0.64, GPT-5 Mini 0.64, Gemini 3 Flash 0.64, GPT-5.2 0.63, GPT-5 Nano 0.59, Qwen3 8B 0.48, Gemini 3 Pro 0.47, DeepSeek V3.2 0.46, Grok 4.1 Fast 0.35, Qwen3 30B 0.33
Framing effect (explicit payoffs added): Nash accuracy +6.20%, utilitarian accuracy -4.06%
Order effect: coordination baseline 87% (vs 50% random-chance), drops 15% on average under randomized option order; GPT-5 models drop only 5-6%
Reasoning-category deltas (optimal minus suboptimal): Payoff Maximization -0.17, Rawlsian +0.11, AI Safety +0.10, Utilitarian +0.07

Mechanism-design gains in utilitarian accuracy over baseline: Mediator +0.18 (best), others up to +0.13 (Contracts); baseline Nash 0.57, Utilitarian 0.59; Nash trade-off Messages +0.03, Contracts +0.04, Payments/Penalties/Mediator -0.06
Per-model mechanism gains range +0.01 (Llama 3.2 3B) to +0.30 (Grok 4.1) and +0.28 (Gemini 3 Pro, with -0.09 Nash)
Construction quality: filtering pass rate 84.5% (1,535/1,816); structural verification 99.7% (1,530/1,535); human study kappa=0.84, 86.7% agreement (26/30); taxonomy coverage TVD=6.43%
baselines_compared: No-mechanism baseline (all 1,535 games, no intervention): Nash 0.57, Utilitarian 0.59
Naturalistic vs explicit-payoff framing; default vs randomized option ordering
15 frontier models across closed (GPT, Claude, Gemini, Grok) and open-weight (Qwen3, DeepSeek, LLaMA3) families as comparison subjects
Prior benchmarks positioned against in Table 1 (not re-run): Akata et al. (144 instances), GTBench (10), TMGBench (720), FAIRGAME (2), Lore & Heydari (5), SanctSim (1), MoralSim (6), CoopEval (4)
